expanded_description:
Quantitative easing (QE) is an unconventional monetary policy tool used by central banks when conventional policy—lowering short-term interest rates—is no longer effective because rates have already reached the zero lower bound (ZLB). Under QE, the central bank purchases large quantities of long-term government bonds and other securities (such as mortgage-backed securities or corporate bonds) to lower long-term interest rates, ease financial conditions, and stimulate the economy. QE expands the central bank's balance sheet dramatically and is often described as 'money printing,' though in practice it is electronic reserve creation.

QE was first deployed by the Bank of Japan in the early 2000s, but it became a mainstream tool after the 2008 global financial crisis, when the Federal Reserve, the Bank of England, the European Central Bank, and the Bank of Canada (though Canada used QE less aggressively) all implemented large-scale asset purchase programs. During the 2020 COVID-19 pandemic, QE was used again on an even larger scale. The Bank of Canada, while historically more cautious about QE, engaged in its first major QE program in 2020, purchasing government bonds to support financial markets and lower borrowing costs.

Understanding QE is essential for analyzing how central banks respond to severe recessions and financial crises, evaluating the effectiveness and risks of unconventional policy, and interpreting central bank balance sheet dynamics. In this section, we will examine the mechanics of QE, the transmission channels through which QE affects the economy (portfolio balance channel, signaling channel, liquidity channel), the evidence on QE effectiveness, the risks and side effects (inflation, asset bubbles, exit challenges), and the differences in QE implementation across central banks (Fed, BoC, ECB, BOJ). You will learn to analyze QE announcements, calculate the impact on long-term yields, evaluate the trade-offs of large-scale asset purchases, and understand the process of quantitative tightening (QT) when QE is reversed.

core_concept.principle:
Quantitative easing (QE) is an unconventional monetary policy tool in which the central bank purchases long-term securities to lower long-term interest rates and ease financial conditions when short-term rates are at the zero lower bound. QE works through three main channels:

1. Portfolio Balance Channel:
• Central bank buys long-term bonds, removing them from private portfolios.
• Investors who sell bonds reinvest the proceeds in other assets (stocks, corporate bonds, real estate), driving up prices and lowering yields across asset classes.
• This reduces term premiums (the extra yield investors demand for holding long-term bonds) and lowers long-term interest rates.
• Estimated effect: \$1 trillion of QE lowers the 10-year Treasury yield by 0.2-0.5 percentage points (Fed studies).

2. Signaling Channel:
• QE signals that the central bank will keep short-term rates low for an extended period (commitment to accommodation).
• This reduces market expectations of future short-term rates, further lowering long-term yields.
• The signaling effect is strongest when QE is announced unexpectedly.

3. Liquidity Channel (Market Functioning):
• In a crisis, markets can become illiquid (e.g., MBS market froze in 2008, corporate bond market froze in 2020).
• QE purchases restore liquidity by providing a buyer of last resort, reducing bid-ask spreads and allowing markets to function.
• This channel is distinct from the portfolio balance channel and operates quickly.

QE Programs by Major Central Banks:
• Federal Reserve (Fed): QE1 (2008-2010, \$1.75T), QE2 (2010-2011, \$600B), QE3 (2012-2014, \$85B/month), QE4 (2020-2022, \$120B/month).
• Bank of Canada (BoC): 2009 (no major QE), 2020-2021 (first major QE, purchased \$5B/week of government bonds, balance sheet grew from \$120B to \$570B).
• European Central Bank (ECB): Securities Markets Programme (2010-2012), Outright Monetary Transactions (2012), Asset Purchase Programme (2015-2018, \$80B/month), Pandemic Emergency Purchase Programme (2020-2021, \$1.85T).
• Bank of Japan (BOJ): QE since early 2000s, expanded to Quantitative and Qualitative Easing (QQE) in 2013, Yield Curve Control (YCC) since 2016.

Quantitative Tightening (QT):
• The reverse of QE: allowing securities to mature without reinvestment (passive QT) or actively selling securities.
• QT shrinks the central bank's balance sheet, putting upward pressure on long-term yields.
• Risks: taper tantrum (2013), repo spike (2019).
core_concept.explanation:
Imagine the economy is in a deep recession. The Fed has already cut the federal funds rate to zero, but borrowing costs are still too high because long-term interest rates (like mortgage rates and corporate bond yields) are elevated. The Fed cannot cut short-term rates further. So it turns to QE.

The Fed announces it will buy \$100 billion per month of 10-year Treasury bonds and mortgage-backed securities. It creates electronic reserves to pay for them. The increased demand for these bonds pushes their prices up and yields down. The 10-year Treasury yield falls from 3.5% to 2.5%. Mortgage rates, which are tied to Treasury yields, fall from 5.5% to 4.5%. A family can now afford a larger mortgage, boosting housing demand. A corporation can issue bonds at lower rates, financing new investment.

Investors who sold their Treasuries to the Fed now have cash. They need to reinvest it. Some buy stocks, pushing stock prices up. Others buy corporate bonds, pushing corporate yields down. Others buy real estate. The portfolio balance channel spreads the impact of QE across all asset classes.

In Canada, the BoC had never used QE before 2020. When the pandemic hit, the BoC announced it would buy \$5 billion per week of government bonds. The 10-year Canadian government bond yield fell from 1.5% to 0.5%. Mortgage rates fell, housing boomed (perhaps too much), and the economy recovered. The BoC ended QE in October 2021 and began QT in April 2022.

QE is powerful but not without risks. Too much QE can cause inflation (as seen in 2021-2022). It can fuel asset bubbles (housing, stocks, crypto). And exiting QE (QT) is difficult—the 2013 'taper tantrum' showed that markets react violently when the Fed hints at reducing purchases.

image.alt: Diagram: Central bank creates reserves → buys long-term bonds from investors → investors reinvest in other assets → asset prices rise, yields fall → lower borrowing costs → higher spending. Three channels labeled.
image.caption: Figure 8.6.1: Quantitative easing mechanics: the central bank purchases long-term securities, lowering yields and easing financial conditions through portfolio balance, signaling, and liquidity channels.

sections.introduction:
Quantitative easing (QE) is an unconventional monetary policy tool used by central banks when conventional policy—lowering short-term interest rates—is no longer effective because rates have already reached the zero lower bound (ZLB). Under QE, the central bank purchases large quantities of long-term government bonds and other securities (such as mortgage-backed securities or corporate bonds) to lower long-term interest rates, ease financial conditions, and stimulate the economy. QE expands the central bank's balance sheet dramatically and is often described as 'money printing,' though in practice it is electronic reserve creation.

QE was first deployed by the Bank of Japan in the early 2000s, but it became a mainstream tool after the 2008 global financial crisis, when the Federal Reserve, the Bank of England, the European Central Bank, and the Bank of Canada (though Canada used QE less aggressively) all implemented large-scale asset purchase programs. During the 2020 COVID-19 pandemic, QE was used again on an even larger scale. The Bank of Canada, while historically more cautious about QE, engaged in its first major QE program in 2020, purchasing government bonds to support financial markets and lower borrowing costs.

In this section, we will examine the mechanics of QE, the transmission channels through which QE affects the economy (portfolio balance channel, signaling channel, liquidity channel), the evidence on QE effectiveness, the risks and side effects (inflation, asset bubbles, exit challenges), and the differences in QE implementation across central banks (Fed, BoC, ECB, BOJ).

introduction_image.alt: Timeline: 2001 (BOJ first QE), 2008-2014 (Fed QE1-3), 2010-2012 (ECM SMP/OMT), 2015-2018 (ECB APP), 2020 (Fed QE4, BoC QE, ECB PEPP).
introduction_image.caption: Figure 8.6.2: Historical timeline of quantitative easing programs across major central banks; QE became a mainstream tool after the 2008 crisis.

key_concept.definition:
Quantitative Easing (QE): An unconventional monetary policy in which a central bank purchases long-term securities to lower long-term interest rates and ease financial conditions when short-term rates are at the zero lower bound.

Transmission Channels:

1. Portfolio Balance Channel:
• Central bank buys long-term bonds, reducing supply available to private investors.
• Investors reinvest proceeds in other assets (stocks, corporate bonds, real estate), driving up prices and lowering yields.
• Term premium (compensation for interest rate risk) falls, lowering long-term yields.
• Effect size: diminishing returns (first \$100B has larger effect than next \$100B).

2. Signaling Channel:
• QE signals that the central bank will keep short-term rates low for an extended period.
• Reduces expected future short-term rates, lowering long-term yields.
• Credibility matters: if markets doubt commitment, signaling effect is weak.

3. Liquidity Channel:
• In a crisis, markets become illiquid (wide bid-ask spreads, no buyers).
• QE provides a buyer of last resort, restoring market functioning.
• This channel is distinct from portfolio balance and operates quickly.

QE Programs by Central Bank:

Federal Reserve:
• QE1 (2008-2010): \$1.75T (MBS, Treasuries, agency debt)
• QE2 (2010-2011): \$600B (Treasuries)
• QE3 (2012-2014): \$85B/month (Treasuries, MBS)
• QE4 (2020-2022): \$120B/month (\$80B Treasuries, \$40B MBS)

Bank of Canada:
• 2009: No major QE (Canada recovered faster, banking system stable)
• 2020-2021: First major QE, \$5B/week of government bonds (later reduced to \$2B/week)
• Balance sheet grew from \$120B to \$570B
• QE ended October 2021; QT began April 2022

European Central Bank:
• SMP (2010-2012): \$220B
• OMT (2012): unlimited bond purchases (not activated)
• APP (2015-2018): \$80B/month
• PEPP (2020-2021): \$1.85T

Bank of Japan:
• QE (2001-2006): first central bank to use QE
• QQE (2013-present): unlimited purchases
• YCC (2016-present): cap on 10-year JGB yield at 0%

Quantitative Tightening (QT):
• Reverse of QE: central bank allows securities to mature without reinvestment (passive QT) or sells securities (active QT).
• QT raises long-term yields by reversing portfolio balance channel.
• Risks: taper tantrum (2013), repo spike (2019).
key_concept.context: The concept of QE dates back to economist John Maynard Keynes, who suggested that central banks could buy long-term bonds to lower long-term rates when short rates were at zero. The Bank of Japan first implemented QE in 2001 to combat deflation. The 2008 crisis brought QE to the US, UK, and Eurozone. The Fed's QE programs added \$3.5 trillion to its balance sheet. The BoC, historically a 'reluctant' user of QE, deployed it for the first time in 2020. Research on QE effectiveness suggests that Fed QE lowered the 10-year Treasury yield by 1-1.5 percentage points cumulatively. The BoC's QE lowered Canadian yields by 0.5-1.0 percentage points. The main risks of QE are inflation (which materialized in 2021-2022), asset bubbles (housing, stocks), and exit challenges (taper tantrum). The debate over QE continues: some argue it was essential to avoid depression; others argue it fueled inequality and inflation.

image.alt: Graph: Fed balance sheet size (2007-2024) from \$0.9T to \$9T; BoC balance sheet (2019-2024) from \$120B to \$570B; QE episodes marked.
image.caption: Figure 8.6.3: QE dramatically expanded central bank balance sheets; the Fed's balance sheet grew from \$0.9T to \$9T; the BoC's from \$120B to \$570B.

classifications.type: Portfolio Balance Channel: The Primary Transmission Mechanism
classifications.value: QE lowers long-term yields by removing bonds from private portfolios and forcing investors to buy other assets
characteristics: Central bank buys long-term bonds → reduces supply available to private investors
Investors who sell bonds have cash and must reinvest → buy stocks, corporate bonds, real estate
Increased demand for other assets raises their prices, lowering yields (portfolio rebalancing)
Term premium (compensation for interest rate risk) falls as duration is removed from market
Effect diminishes with scale (diminishing returns): first \$100B has larger effect than next \$100B
Estimated effect: \$1T of QE lowers 10-year yield by 0.2-0.5 percentage points (Fed studies)
classifications.behavior:
The portfolio balance channel is the primary mechanism through which QE affects the economy. When the central bank buys long-term bonds, it removes duration (interest rate risk) from the market. Private investors who held those bonds now have cash, which they must reinvest. They have a choice: buy other long-term bonds (driving yields down further), buy corporate bonds (driving credit spreads down), buy stocks (driving equity prices up), or buy real estate (driving property prices up). The result is a broad easing of financial conditions across all asset classes.

Empirical research on the Fed's QE programs suggests that QE1 (\$1.75T) lowered the 10-year Treasury yield by about 1.0-1.5 percentage points. Subsequent QE rounds had smaller effects (diminishing returns). QE2 (\$600B) lowered yields by 0.2-0.3 percentage points. QE3 (\$85B/month) had an estimated effect of 0.1-0.2 percentage points per month. The BoC's 2020 QE (\$5B/week) lowered the 10-year Canadian government bond yield by 0.5-1.0 percentage points.

The portfolio balance channel also works through corporate bonds. When the Fed or BoC buys government bonds, investors shift into corporate bonds, lowering credit spreads. This is especially important for businesses that rely on bond financing. The Fed's 2020 corporate bond purchases (SMCCF) directly targeted this channel. The BoC's 2020 Corporate Bond Purchase Program (CBPP) also directly purchased corporate bonds.

image.alt: Diagram: Fed buys Treasuries → investors sell Treasuries, get cash → reinvest in corporate bonds, stocks, real estate → prices rise, yields fall → lower borrowing costs for firms and households.
image.caption: Figure 8.6.4: The portfolio balance channel: QE removes bonds from private portfolios, forcing investors to rebalance into other assets, easing financial conditions broadly.

examples.explanation: The Fed announced QE1 in November 2008, committing to buy \$600 billion of MBS and agency debt. The 10-year Treasury yield fell from 4.0% to 2.5% over the following months—a 150 basis point decline. Research by Gagnon et al. (2011) estimated that QE1 lowered the 10-year yield by 1.2 percentage points through the portfolio balance channel.

examples.process: BoC QE (2020) Effect on Canadian Yields
examples.explanation: The BoC announced its first major QE program in March 2020, committing to buy \$5 billion per week of government bonds. The 10-year Canadian government bond yield fell from 1.5% to 0.5% over the following months. The BoC estimated that QE lowered yields by 0.6-1.0 percentage points, with most of the effect coming from the portfolio balance channel.

classifications.value: QE signals that the central bank will keep short-term rates low for an extended period, lowering expected future rates
characteristics: QE announcement signals central bank's commitment to accommodative policy
Reduces market expectations of future short-term rates, lowering long-term yields
Signaling effect is strongest when QE is announced unexpectedly
More powerful when central bank has credibility (markets believe the commitment)
Can work even if the central bank does not purchase many bonds (small QE can signal big commitment)
Example: Fed's 2012 QE3 announcement had large signaling effect
classifications.behavior:
The signaling channel operates through expectations. When a central bank announces QE, it is not just buying bonds—it is also telling markets that it will keep short-term rates low for a long time. This reduces the expected path of future short-term rates, which lowers long-term yields (since long-term yields are averages of expected future short rates plus term premiums).

The signaling channel is most powerful when QE is announced unexpectedly. If markets already expect the central bank to be accommodative, the signaling effect is smaller. The Fed's 2012 QE3 announcement (open-ended purchases) had a large signaling effect because markets were uncertain about the Fed's commitment. The BoC's 2020 QE announcement also had a signaling effect, though the BoC had already cut rates to 0.25%, so the additional signal was smaller.

Credibility is crucial for the signaling channel. If markets doubt that the central bank will follow through on its commitment, the signaling effect is weak. The Fed's credibility was high in 2020 (after decades of successful inflation targeting), so its signaling was effective. The ECB's 2012 OMT announcement (to buy unlimited bonds of distressed Eurozone countries) had a powerful signaling effect even though OMT was never actually used—markets believed the ECB would act if needed. This is the ultimate signaling channel: commitment without purchases.

image.alt: Graph: Expected path of federal funds rate before and after QE announcement; announcement lowers expected future rates, reducing long-term yields.
image.caption: Figure 8.6.5: The signaling channel: QE announcements lower market expectations of future short-term rates, reducing long-term yields.

examples.explanation: The Fed announced open-ended QE3 (\$40B/month MBS, later \$85B/month total) and explicitly tied it to labor market conditions ('substantial improvement'). This forward guidance combined with QE had a large signaling effect: the 10-year Treasury yield fell from 1.8% to 1.6% on the announcement, and expected future rates fell significantly. Research suggests the signaling channel accounted for 30-50% of the total effect of QE3.

The ECB announced Outright Monetary Transactions (OMT): it would buy unlimited bonds of Eurozone countries facing speculative attacks, subject to conditionality. OMT was never actually used, but the announcement alone lowered Spanish and Italian bond yields by 2-3 percentage points. The signaling channel was extraordinarily powerful because markets believed the ECB would act. This is the purest example of the signaling channel.

classifications.value: In a crisis, QE provides a buyer of last resort, restoring liquidity to dysfunctional markets
characteristics: During crises, markets can freeze (no buyers, wide bid-ask spreads)
QE purchases provide a backstop buyer, restoring market functioning
This channel is distinct from portfolio balance (which focuses on yields)
Operates quickly (within days) as markets anticipate central bank purchases
Examples: MBS market in 2008, corporate bond market in 2020
BoC's 2020 QE included purchases of provincial bonds and corporate bonds to restore liquidity
classifications.behavior:
The liquidity channel is often overlooked but was crucial in 2008 and 2020. In a financial crisis, markets can freeze: investors are unwilling to buy assets because of uncertainty, leading to wide bid-ask spreads and fire sales. The central bank can act as a 'buyer of last resort,' purchasing assets to restore liquidity.

In 2008, the MBS market froze. The Fed's announcement that it would buy MBS (QE1) immediately restored liquidity, even before significant purchases occurred. Bid-ask spreads narrowed, and prices stabilized. This prevented a cascade of fire sales that would have deepened the recession.

In 2020, the corporate bond market froze. The Fed announced the Primary Market Corporate Credit Facility (PMCCF) and Secondary Market Corporate Credit Facility (SMCCF) to buy corporate bonds. The BoC announced the Corporate Bond Purchase Program (CBPP). These announcements alone restored liquidity, even before purchases began. The liquidity channel was the primary channel in the immediate crisis response.

The liquidity channel is distinct from the portfolio balance channel. It focuses on market functioning rather than yields. It operates quickly (within days) because markets anticipate central bank purchases. It is particularly important for assets that are not normally purchased by central banks (corporate bonds, provincial bonds, commercial paper).

image.alt: Graph: Bid-ask spread for MBS (2008) and corporate bonds (2020); spreads spike during crisis, fall when QE announced; liquidity restored.
image.caption: Figure 8.6.6: The liquidity channel: QE announcements reduce bid-ask spreads and restore market functioning during crises.

examples.explanation: In November 2008, the Fed announced it would buy \$600 billion of MBS. The MBS market had been frozen (bid-ask spreads over 1%). The announcement alone reduced spreads to 0.1% within days. The Fed's commitment to purchase MBS provided a backstop, allowing private investors to re-enter the market. The liquidity channel was critical to stabilizing the housing market.

In March 2020, the BoC announced the Corporate Bond Purchase Program (CBPP) to buy corporate bonds in secondary markets. Canadian corporate bond spreads had widened dramatically (from 1% to 4%). The announcement alone reduced spreads to 2% within days. The BoC purchased \$10 billion of corporate bonds, but most of the effect came from the announcement, not the actual purchases. This is the liquidity channel at work.

classifications.value: QT is the process of shrinking the central bank's balance sheet, reversing the effects of QE
characteristics: Passive QT: Allowing securities to mature without reinvesting proceeds
Active QT: Selling securities outright (rare, as it could disrupt markets)
QT raises long-term yields by reversing the portfolio balance channel
Risks: taper tantrum (markets react violently to QT announcement)
Risks: repo spike (liquidity shortage as reserves are drained)
Fed QT: 2017-2019 (ended early due to repo spike), 2022-present
BoC QT: 2022-present (allowing bonds to mature without reinvestment)
classifications.behavior:
Quantitative tightening (QT) is the reverse of QE. When the central bank allows bonds to mature without reinvesting the proceeds (passive QT), its balance sheet shrinks. This puts upward pressure on long-term yields as the portfolio balance channel reverses: the central bank is no longer absorbing duration, so private investors must hold more long-term bonds, pushing yields up.

The Fed's first QT experiment (2017-2019) was cut short by the September 2019 repo spike. The Fed had drained too many reserves from the banking system, causing a liquidity shortage and a spike in overnight rates to 10%. The lesson: QT must be managed carefully to avoid creating liquidity shortages. The Fed now has a Standing Repo Facility (SRF) to backstop liquidity.

The BoC began QT in April 2022, allowing government bonds to mature without reinvestment. The BoC's balance sheet shrank from \$570B to \$300B by mid-2024. Unlike the Fed's first QT, the BoC's QT did not cause a repo spike because the BoC maintained ample settlement balances.

The 'taper tantrum' of 2013 showed that even the announcement of future QT (or tapering of QE) can cause market turmoil. When then-Fed Chair Bernanke hinted in May 2013 that the Fed might reduce its QE purchases, the 10-year Treasury yield spiked from 1.6% to 2.6% in two months. The Fed learned to communicate QT more carefully, using forward guidance to prepare markets.

image.alt: Graph: Fed balance sheet (2008-2024) showing QE expansions and QT contractions; 10-year yield during QT periods; 2013 taper tantrum marked.
image.caption: Figure 8.6.7: Quantitative tightening shrinks the balance sheet and raises long-term yields; the 2013 taper tantrum showed that QT announcements can cause market turmoil.

examples.explanation: On May 22, 2013, Fed Chair Bernanke testified that the Fed might 'reduce the pace of asset purchases' in coming meetings. Markets reacted violently: the 10-year Treasury yield rose from 1.6% to 2.6% over two months (a 100 basis point increase). Mortgage rates spiked, and emerging markets saw capital outflows. The Fed delayed tapering until December 2013 and communicated more carefully thereafter. The taper tantrum taught central banks that exit from QE must be telegraphed well in advance.

The Fed's QT had drained reserves from \$2.5T to \$1.5T. In September 2019, a confluence of corporate tax payments and Treasury coupon settlements caused a sudden demand for reserves. Overnight repo rates spiked to 10% (while the fed funds target was 2.25%). The Fed had to inject \$100B in reserves via emergency repos. The lesson: QT must not drain reserves too much; central banks need to maintain ample reserves to avoid liquidity shortages. The Fed created the Standing Repo Facility (SRF) in 2021 to prevent a repeat.

principles.principle: QE Has Diminishing Returns; The First Round Is Most Effective
principles.statement: Empirical research across multiple central banks shows that the marginal effect of QE declines with each additional round. The first \$100 billion of QE has a larger effect on long-term yields than the second \$100 billion, and so on. This is because the portfolio balance channel saturates: once the central bank has removed most of the duration from the market, additional purchases have little effect. The Fed's QE1 (2008-2010) lowered the 10-year yield by 1.0-1.5 percentage points; QE2 (2010-2011) lowered it by 0.2-0.3 percentage points; QE3 (2012-2014) had even smaller effects. Similarly, the BoC's 2020 QE had a large initial effect (lowering yields by 1.0 percentage points), but the marginal effect declined as the program continued. Diminishing returns imply that there is an optimal scale for QE: enough to lower yields significantly, but not so much that the costs (inflation risk, exit difficulty) outweigh the benefits.
principles.application: Central banks should calibrate QE to achieve specific yield targets (e.g., 10-year yield at 1%) rather than setting arbitrary purchase amounts. The Fed's 2020 QE (unlimited) recognized diminishing returns by emphasizing that purchases would continue until 'substantial further progress' was made, not a fixed amount. When evaluating QE programs, analysts should focus on the marginal effect per dollar of purchases, not the cumulative effect. Diminishing returns also imply that the first round of QE is most important; subsequent rounds are less cost-effective.
principles.example: The Fed's QE1 (\$1.75T) lowered the 10-year Treasury yield by 1.2 percentage points. QE2 (\$600B) lowered it by 0.3 percentage points. The marginal effect of QE2 was 0.05 percentage points per \$100B, compared to 0.07 for QE1—a 30% decline. QE3 had even smaller marginal effects (0.02-0.03 per \$100B). The BoC's 2020 QE: the first \$100B lowered Canadian yields by 0.5 percentage points; the next \$100B lowered them by 0.2 percentage points. Diminishing returns are a robust finding across countries.

image.alt: Graph: Cumulative QE purchases (x-axis) vs. reduction in 10-year yield (y-axis); curve flattens as purchases increase, showing diminishing returns.
image.caption: Figure 8.6.8: QE exhibits diminishing returns: the first purchases have the largest effect on yields; additional purchases have smaller marginal effects.

principles.principle: QE Can Cause Inflation and Asset Bubbles if Overused
principles.statement: While QE did not cause inflation in the 2009-2019 period (inflation averaged 1.5%), the massive QE programs of 2020-2021 (combined with fiscal stimulus) did contribute to the 2021-2022 inflation surge. The Fed's balance sheet grew from \$4T to \$9T; the BoC's from \$120B to \$570B. When combined with supply chain disruptions and energy shocks, the excess liquidity contributed to demand-pull inflation. QE can also fuel asset bubbles: low interest rates encourage investors to search for yield, driving up prices of stocks, real estate, and crypto. The 2020-2021 housing boom in Canada and the US was partly fueled by QE-driven low mortgage rates. Central banks must balance the benefits of QE (supporting the economy during crises) against the risks of overuse (inflation, bubbles, financial instability). The key is to exit QE (QT) in a timely manner once the economy recovers.
principles.application: Central banks should not treat QE as cost-free. They should have clear exit criteria (e.g., inflation above target, unemployment below natural rate) and communicate them in advance. The Fed's 2013 taper tantrum showed that markets react violently to poorly communicated exits; the BoC's 2021-2022 exit was smoother because it communicated clearly. When QE is combined with large fiscal stimulus (as in 2020-2021), the risk of inflation is higher; central banks must be prepared to tighten more aggressively. The 2021-2022 inflation surge taught central banks that QE is not neutral—it can cause inflation if overused.
principles.example: In Canada, the BoC's QE (\$5B/week from March 2020 to October 2021) contributed to a housing boom: the Teranet Home Price Index rose 40% from 2020 to 2022. Low mortgage rates (fueled by QE) were a primary driver. Inflation also surged to 8% in 2022. The BoC had to raise rates aggressively (to 5%) and end QE to cool the economy. The lesson: QE is powerful but can have side effects. The BoC's post-2022 QT has been relatively smooth, but the housing market remains elevated.

image.alt: Graph: Fed balance sheet size (2008-2024) vs. inflation (CPI); 2009-2019: balance sheet up, inflation low; 2020-2022: balance sheet up, inflation up; correlation not perfect but QE contributed.
image.caption: Figure 8.6.9: QE did not cause inflation in the 2009-2019 period, but the massive 2020-2021 QE (combined with fiscal stimulus) contributed to the 2021-2022 inflation surge.

worked_examples.title: Calculating the Effect of QE on Long-Term Yields (Portfolio Balance Channel)

image.alt: Table: QE amount $1T, elasticity -0.3, yield change = -0.3 × (1T/20T) = -1.5%? Actually calculating correctly: yield change = -0.3 × (1/20) = -0.015 = -1.5 percentage points? That seems too large; correct calculation shown.
image.caption: Figure 8.6.10: Worked example: estimating the effect of QE on the 10-year Treasury yield using the portfolio balance channel.

worked_examples.problem:
A central bank is considering a QE program. Research suggests that the portfolio balance channel implies that the 10-year government bond yield changes according to: $\Delta i = -\beta \times \frac{QE}{GDP}$, where $\beta$ is the elasticity (estimated at 0.3 for the first \$100B, diminishing thereafter). GDP = \$25 trillion.

a) Calculate the expected reduction in the 10-year yield for QE of \$500 billion (2% of GDP) assuming constant elasticity.
b) If diminishing returns reduce the elasticity to 0.15 for the next \$500 billion (another 2% of GDP), calculate the total reduction for \$1 trillion QE.
c) If the 10-year yield is initially 3.0%, what is the yield after \$1 trillion QE?
d) The central bank wants to lower the 10-year yield to 1.5%. How much QE is needed (assuming the same diminishing returns structure)?
e) Why might the actual effect be different from this calculation?

step_by_step.explanation:
QE as % of GDP = $500B / $25T = 0.02 = 2%

Assuming constant elasticity β = 0.3:
$\Delta i = -0.3 \times 2\% = -0.6 percentage points

The 10-year yield falls by 0.6 percentage points (e.g., from 3.0% to 2.4%).

step_by_step.step: Calculate effect of second $500B QE (diminishing returns)
step_by_step.explanation:
For the second \$500B (another 2% of GDP), the elasticity falls to β = 0.15.

$\Delta i = -0.15 \times 2\% = -0.3 percentage points

Total reduction for \$1T QE = 0.6% + 0.3% = -0.9 percentage points

step_by_step.step: Calculate QE needed to reach 1.5% yield
step_by_step.explanation:
Target reduction = 3.0% - 1.5% = 1.5 percentage points.

Let x be the first portion of QE (up to diminishing returns threshold) and y the second portion. Assume the first 2% of GDP (\$500B) has elasticity 0.3; additional purchases have elasticity 0.15.

We need: 0.3 × (first portion % of GDP) + 0.15 × (additional % of GDP) = 1.5%

Let a = first portion % of GDP (max 2%), b = additional % of GDP.

If a = 2% (\$500B), then contribution from first portion = 0.3 × 2% = 0.6%.
Remaining needed = 1.5% - 0.6% = 0.9%.
0.9% = 0.15 × b → b = 0.9% / 0.15 = 6% of GDP.

Total QE as % of GDP = 2% + 6% = 8% of GDP = 8% × \$25T = \$2 trillion

This is a very large QE program (\$2T), reflecting the diminishing returns: after the first \$500B, additional purchases have much smaller effects. In reality, the Fed's QE4 was about \$4T (unlimited), which achieved a 10-year yield of 0.5% (not 1.5%). The model is simplified; actual effects depend on expectations and other factors.

step_by_step.step: Discuss why actual effect may differ
step_by_step.explanation:
The actual effect of QE on long-term yields may differ from this calculation for several reasons:

1. Signaling channel: The announcement effect (signaling) can be larger than the portfolio balance channel, especially if the QE is unexpected.
2. Liquidity channel: In a crisis, the liquidity channel can dominate; yields may fall sharply even with small purchases.
3. Market expectations: If markets already expect QE, the effect is smaller (priced in).
4. Global factors: Yields are affected by global forces (e.g., European QE affects US yields).
5. Forward guidance: If the central bank also provides forward guidance, the effect is larger.
6. Diminishing returns shape: The elasticity may not be linear; it could decay exponentially, making later purchases even less effective.
7. Term premium variation: The term premium is not constant; other factors (growth expectations, inflation expectations) also affect yields.

In practice, central banks use a combination of models and market reactions to calibrate QE. The Fed's QE4 (2020-2022) was 'unlimited' precisely because the Fed did not know the exact elasticity; it committed to purchases until 'substantial further progress' was made. The BoC's QE (2020-2021) was also open-ended, with the BoC adjusting weekly purchase amounts based on market conditions.

worked_examples.final_answer: a) -0.6 percentage points. b) Total reduction -0.9 percentage points. c) 2.1%. d) \$2 trillion (8% of GDP). e) Signaling, liquidity, expectations, global factors, forward guidance, non-linear diminishing returns, term premium variation.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying the portfolio balance channel of QE to estimate the impact on long-term yields, accounting for diminishing returns.

worked_examples.title: Comparing QE Programs: Fed vs. Bank of Canada

image.alt: Table: Fed QE4 ($4T, 120B/month, 10-year yield -1.5%); BoC QE ($450B, 5B/week, 10-year yield -1.0%); differences in scale and impact.
image.caption: Figure 8.6.11: Worked example: comparing the scale, duration, and effectiveness of Fed and BoC QE programs during the 2020 pandemic.

worked_examples.problem:
Compare the QE programs of the Federal Reserve and the Bank of Canada during the 2020 COVID-19 pandemic.

Federal Reserve:
• QE4 announced March 2020: unlimited purchases, initially \$120B/month (\$80B Treasuries, \$40B MBS)
• Balance sheet grew from \$4.2T to \$8.9T (peak)
• 10-year Treasury yield fell from 1.5% (pre-QE) to 0.5% (lowest)
• QE ended March 2022 (tapered to zero)

Bank of Canada:
• QE announced March 2020: \$5B/week of government bonds (later reduced to \$2B/week, then \$1B/week)
• Balance sheet grew from \$120B to \$570B (peak)
• 10-year Canadian government bond yield fell from 1.5% to 0.5%
• QE ended October 2021 (tapered to zero)

a) Calculate the increase in the Fed's balance sheet as a percentage of GDP (US GDP = \$25T).
b) Calculate the increase in the BoC's balance sheet as a percentage of GDP (Canada GDP = \$2.2T).
c) Calculate the reduction in the 10-year yield for each central bank (in basis points).
d) Compute the 'efficiency' of QE (yield reduction per \$1 of balance sheet expansion as % of GDP). Which QE was more efficient?
e) Why might the BoC's QE have been more efficient despite being smaller relative to GDP?

step_by_step.step: Calculate Fed balance sheet increase as % of GDP
step_by_step.explanation:
Fed balance sheet increase = $8.9T - $4.2T = \$4.7 trillion

US GDP = \$25 trillion

Increase as % of GDP = $4.7T / $25T = 18.8%

step_by_step.step: Calculate BoC balance sheet increase as % of GDP
step_by_step.explanation:
BoC balance sheet increase = $570B - $120B = \$450 billion

Canada GDP = \$2.2 trillion

Increase as % of GDP = $450B / $2.2T = 20.5%

Fed: 10-year Treasury yield fell from 1.5% to 0.5% = -1.0 percentage point (100 basis points)

BoC: 10-year Canadian yield fell from 1.5% to 0.5% = -1.0 percentage point (100 basis points)

Both achieved a 100 basis point reduction in long-term yields.

step_by_step.step: Compute efficiency (yield reduction per $1 of balance sheet expansion as % of GDP)
step_by_step.explanation:
Efficiency = (Yield reduction in percentage points) / (Balance sheet increase as % of GDP)

Fed efficiency = 1.0 / 18.8% = 0.053 percentage points per 1% of GDP

BoC efficiency = 1.0 / 20.5% = 0.049 percentage points per 1% of GDP

The Fed's QE was slightly more efficient (0.053 vs. 0.049), but the difference is small. Both achieved similar yield reductions per unit of balance sheet expansion.

Alternative calculation: Yield reduction per \$1T of balance sheet expansion (absolute).

Fed: 1.0% / $4.7T = 0.21 percentage points per \$1T
BoC: 1.0% / $0.45T = 2.22 percentage points per \$1T

In absolute terms, the BoC's QE appears far more efficient (2.22 vs. 0.21). But this is because Canada's economy is smaller; the same \$1T of purchases would be 45% of Canada's GDP (impossible). The relative measure (% of GDP) is more appropriate for comparing across countries.

step_by_step.step: Explain why BoC's QE might have been more efficient (relative measure shows similar; but other factors)
step_by_step.explanation:
While the efficiency measures are similar (0.053 vs. 0.049), several factors could explain differences:

1. Market structure: Canada's bond market is smaller and less liquid than the US market, so the same relative purchase has a larger effect.
2. Signaling channel: The BoC's QE was its first major QE program, so the announcement had a larger signaling effect (markets were surprised). The Fed had already done QE1-3, so QE4 was less surprising.
3. Fiscal policy coordination: Canada's fiscal stimulus (CERB, wage subsidies) was larger relative to GDP than US stimulus, amplifying the effect of QE.
4. Exchange rate channel: The BoC's QE depreciated the CAD, boosting exports; the Fed's QE depreciated the USD less because other central banks were also doing QE.
5. Housing market sensitivity: Canada's housing market is more sensitive to interest rates (variable-rate mortgages), so the same yield reduction has a larger effect on the economy.

However, the similar efficiency measures suggest that QE works similarly across countries when scaled to GDP. The key difference is not efficiency but scale: the Fed's QE was larger in absolute terms (\$4.7T vs. \$450B), reflecting the larger US economy.

Both central banks successfully lowered long-term yields by about 100 basis points, supporting the recovery from the pandemic. The BoC ended QE earlier (October 2021 vs. March 2022) because the Canadian economy recovered faster (housing boom, commodity prices).

worked_examples.final_answer: a) Fed: 18.8% of GDP. b) BoC: 20.5% of GDP. c) Both: 100 basis points. d) Fed: 0.053 per 1% of GDP; BoC: 0.049 per 1% of GDP (similar). e) Market structure, signaling (first-time QE), fiscal coordination, exchange rate, housing sensitivity.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Comparing QE programs across countries, scaling by GDP, and evaluating efficiency in terms of yield reduction per unit of balance sheet expansion.

process_flow.process: Central bank identifies ZLB constraint and need for additional stimulus
process_flow.description: Policy rate is at or near zero, but the economy remains weak (recession, deflation risk). Conventional easing exhausted.
process_flow.mechanism: Indicators: policy rate at ZLB, output gap negative, inflation below target, financial markets stressed.

image.alt: Diagram: Policy rate at 0%, output gap negative, inflation below target → decision to implement QE.
image.caption: Figure 8.6.12: Step 1 - The central bank identifies that conventional policy is exhausted and QE is needed.

process_flow.process: Central bank announces QE program (size, duration, asset types)
process_flow.description: The central bank announces it will purchase long-term securities (government bonds, MBS, corporate bonds).
process_flow.mechanism: Announcement includes: total size (e.g., \$600B), pace (e.g., \$80B/month), asset types, and forward guidance about duration. Announcement itself has signaling and liquidity effects.

image.alt: Diagram: QE announcement → signaling channel (lower expected future rates) + liquidity channel (restores market functioning).
image.caption: Figure 8.6.13: Step 2 - QE announcement provides signaling and liquidity effects even before purchases begin.

process_flow.process: Central bank purchases securities, expanding balance sheet
process_flow.description: The central bank creates reserves to buy bonds from primary dealers and other investors.
process_flow.mechanism: Purchases remove duration from the market, triggering portfolio rebalancing. Balance sheet expands. Purchases continue as announced (e.g., weekly or monthly).

image.alt: Diagram: Central bank buys bonds → reserves created → balance sheet expands → duration removed from market.
image.caption: Figure 8.6.14: Step 3 - Actual purchases expand the balance sheet and activate the portfolio balance channel.

process_flow.process: Portfolio balance channel lowers long-term yields and eases financial conditions
process_flow.description: Investors reinvest proceeds in other assets, lowering yields across asset classes (stocks, corporate bonds, real estate).
process_flow.mechanism: Portfolio rebalancing: cash from bond sales flows into other assets, raising prices and lowering yields. Term premiums fall. Financial conditions ease broadly.

image.alt: Diagram: Investors sell bonds to central bank → get cash → reinvest in stocks, corporate bonds, real estate → asset prices rise, yields fall → borrowing costs fall.
image.caption: Figure 8.6.15: Step 4 - The portfolio balance channel spreads the impact of QE across all asset classes, easing financial conditions.

process_flow.process: Eased financial conditions stimulate spending, output, and inflation
process_flow.description: Lower borrowing costs, higher asset prices, and improved market functioning boost aggregate demand.
process_flow.mechanism: Housing, durable goods, business investment, and net exports increase. Output gap closes. Inflation returns to target. Central bank monitors progress and eventually ends QE (tapering) and implements QT.

image.alt: Diagram: Lower yields, higher asset prices → housing, investment, consumption up → AD up → output and inflation up → central bank exits QE (taper, QT).
image.caption: Figure 8.6.16: Step 5 - Eased financial conditions stimulate aggregate demand, closing the output gap and returning inflation to target, leading to QE exit.

image.alt: Master framework: ZLB constraint → QE announcement (signaling/liquidity) → purchases (portfolio balance) → lower yields/eased financial conditions → higher spending → higher output/inflation → exit (taper, QT).
image.caption: Figure 8.6.17: Complete framework for quantitative easing: from ZLB constraint to exit through QT.

key_terms: Federal Reserve QE1, QE2, QE3, QE4
summary: Quantitative easing (QE) is an unconventional monetary policy tool used when short-term interest rates are at the zero lower bound. The central bank purchases long-term securities (government bonds, MBS, corporate bonds) to lower long-term interest rates and ease financial conditions. QE works through three channels: the portfolio balance channel (removing duration from the market forces investors to rebalance into other assets, lowering yields across asset classes), the signaling channel (QE signals that the central bank will keep short-term rates low for an extended period, lowering expected future rates), and the liquidity channel (QE provides a buyer of last resort, restoring market functioning during crises). The Federal Reserve implemented four major QE programs (2008-2014, 2020-2022), adding \$4.7 trillion to its balance sheet. The Bank of Canada implemented its first major QE program in 2020-2021, adding \$450 billion to its balance sheet. Research shows that QE effectively lowers long-term yields (1.0-1.5 percentage points for Fed QE1, 0.5-1.0 for BoC QE), but with diminishing returns (the first purchases have the largest effect). QE carries risks: overuse can cause inflation (as seen in 2021-2022) and fuel asset bubbles (housing, stocks). Exiting QE through quantitative tightening (QT) is challenging, as the 2013 taper tantrum and 2019 repo spike demonstrated. Understanding QE is essential for analyzing how central banks respond to severe recessions and financial crises, evaluating the effectiveness and risks of unconventional policy, and interpreting central bank balance sheet dynamics.
